main_summary.summary: Lt. Gen. Julius W. Becton Jr., interviewed December 15, 2015 by David Cline (Virginia Tech / Southern Oral History Program) for the Library of Congress Civil Rights History Project, traces a career that spans the Army's transition from Jim Crow segregation to integration. Born in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania and a 1944 graduate of Lower Merion High School, Becton enlisted in the Army Air Corps hoping to become a Tuskegee Airman, failed the eye exam at Keesler Field, and was redirected to MacDill Field and then to Officer Candidate School at Fort Benning, Georgia in 1944-1945. He served with the segregated 93rd Infantry Division on Morotai alongside the Dixie Division, deployed to Korea with the 9th Infantry Regiment's 3rd Battalion (which included future Congressman Charles B. Rangel), was twice wounded at Pusan Perimeter and Hill 201, and earned a Silver Star. Becton describes Truman's Executive Order 9981 being read at Aberdeen Proving Ground in 1948 with the post commander declaring no change while present; integration came through battlefield military necessity in Korea under Col. C.C. Sloan rather than executive fiat. He commanded at every level through VII Corps in Germany, retired in 1983, then directed USAID disaster assistance during the African famine, ran FEMA under Reagan and Bush, served as DC Public Schools superintendent during the financial control board era, and chaired the Military Leadership Diversity Commission.
key_themes: Military integration via Executive Order 9981 and Korean War necessity
Career trajectory of Black officers through Jim Crow Army
93rd Infantry Division and Korean War combat
Tuskegee Airmen recruitment and the 99th Pursuit Squadron
Multi-generational Black military leadership (Brooks family, Roscoe Robinson Jr., Roscoe Robinson, Arthur Gray)
main_summary.historical_significance:
Becton is one of the small cohort of Black officers commissioned in the closing months of World War II who served continuously through the segregated Army, integration in Korea, Vietnam, and into four-star-adjacent senior command in Germany. His firsthand account of Officer Candidate School integration at Fort Benning (integrated inside the regiment, segregated immediately upon stepping outside it), Aberdeen Proving Ground's post-commander defiance of Truman's executive order, and Col. C.C. Sloan's decision to assign incoming replacements to the 9th Infantry by MOS rather than race documents the operational mechanics of integration at the unit level.

The interview also preserves contemporaneous testimony about the 1965 Selma march and Alabama National Guard deployment from the Army Operations Center, the Military Leadership Diversity Commission's role in opening Ranger School and combat positions to women, and Becton's tenure at FEMA including the John H. Sununu nuclear-plant licensing dispute that ended his federal career. As the highest-ranking Black combat-arms officer of his cohort to publish on the Black officer pipeline (his Vietnam-era branch chief work assigning all armor officers), his account is a primary source on how the Army produced the Roscoe Robinson and Colin Powell generations.

chapters.title: Bryn Mawr and Lower Merion
chapters.summary: Becton describes his birth in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, his father's work as a janitor at Bryn Mawr College, and growing up on the Main Line in a household one and a half blocks from the college. He attended Lower Merion High School in Ardmore, an integrated school that produced James H. Billington, Alexander Haig, and Henry "Hap" Arnold, while local restaurants, theaters, and swimming programs remained segregated.

chapters.title: Army Air Corps and OCS
chapters.summary: Recruited from Lower Merion's football team in December 1943, Becton enlisted in the Army Air Corps reserves hoping to become a Tuskegee Airman. After failing the eye test at Keesler Field on a new machine-based exam, he was reassigned to MacDill Field's aviation engineering unit. A barely-literate Black first sergeant recommended him for Officer Candidate School; Becton entered Fort Benning, Georgia in January 1945 and was commissioned an infantry second lieutenant in August 1945.
related_events: Army Air Corps reserve induction December 1943

chapters.title: Pacific and Morotai
chapters.summary: Becton joined the 93rd Infantry Division on Morotai in late 1945 alongside the Dixie Division, a unit flying Confederate regimental flags. As a 19-year-old second lieutenant he rode joint Provost Marshal patrols to keep peace between the two divisions and led a patrol into the hills with a Japanese translator attempting to inform holdout Japanese soldiers that the war had ended. He then served as a signal platoon leader in Manila stringing communication poles before the division deactivated in January 1946.

chapters.title: Muhlenberg and Executive Order 9981
chapters.summary: Returning to the States in 1946, Becton enrolled at Muhlenberg College in Allentown on a football scholarship, the first Black student admitted. After a shoulder separation from ill-fitting equipment he focused on pre-med under the GI Bill, married Louise (a nurse from Suffolk) on January 29, 1948. He was at Aberdeen Proving Ground for reserve training in summer 1948 when Truman's Executive Order 9981 was read aloud; the post commander immediately declared no change to segregated officer clubs, swimming pools, and NCO clubs as long as he remained in command.
related_events: Brown v. Board 'all deliberate speed' precedent

chapters.title: Van Horn Incident
chapters.summary: While leading a signal unit convoy from Fort Bliss to Fort Bragg as a junior officer, Becton was chased out of a Van Horn, Texas drug store by an armed sheriff for sitting at the soda fountain in uniform. The next morning his unit took emergency action at a civilian car accident, transporting two injured women to a Van Horn hospital. The same sheriff, learning who saved the women's lives, was visibly chastened, a moment Becton frames as evidence that contact with Black soldiers could shift local racial attitudes.
keywords: Fort Bliss to Fort Bragg convoy

chapters.title: Korea and Hill 201
chapters.summary: Becton deployed to Korea in summer 1950 with the segregated 3rd Battalion, 9th Infantry Regiment (which included future Congressman Charles B. Rangel as an enlisted soldier). He led patrols at Pohang protecting an airfield, broke out of the Pusan Perimeter at Hill 201 where his platoon was caught between Japanese-position fire and a friendly 2nd Battalion engagement, and received his first wound. He earned the Silver Star and two Purple Hearts during the campaign north of the 38th Parallel before being wounded a second time at Thanksgiving 1950 as the Chinese entered the war.
related_events: Chinese entry into Korean War November 1950

chapters.title: Integration by Military Necessity
chapters.summary: Becton describes how the 9th Infantry Regiment integrated in August 1950 when Col. C.C. Sloan ordered incoming replacements assigned by MOS rather than race, accepting a Mexican American soldier from Texas as his first non-Black platoon member. Becton frames integration as a battlefield decision driven by replacement shortages rather than compliance with Executive Order 9981, with Black company commanders eventually leading white-majority battalions. He returned to Camp Edwards in Massachusetts to find one segregated Black company assigned to police duties, complained up the chain, and was reassigned within an hour to Indiantown Gap as a basic training company commander.
related_events: Korean War integration of replacement system 1950-51

chapters.title: Germany and Prairie View
chapters.summary: Becton served as communications officer with the 2nd Armored Division's 42nd Armored Infantry Battalion in Germany, where his mother and mother-in-law visited and Louise's mother stayed long-term. Reassigned to Prairie View A&M University as ROTC instructor (the only captain among a Lt. Colonel and three majors), he simultaneously completed his bachelor's degree in mathematics over the objections of his Professor of Military Science, graduating in 1960 as Prairie View's celebrated 5,000th graduate. His fourth daughter Karen was born during this assignment; she would later become an Air Force Colonel.

chapters.title: Leavenworth and Selma
chapters.summary: After Command and General Staff College at Fort Leavenworth (16 Black officers in a class of 600) Becton was assigned to communications zone in France. While in the Pentagon's Office of National Guard and Reserve Affairs in 1965, he was the duty officer at the Army Operations Center during the Selma to Montgomery march, tasked with extracting deployment data and crew-served weapon locations from a Brigadier General commanding the Alabama National Guard who initially refused to provide them. He frames the era's racial reform as advanced not by participation in marches but by Black officers demonstrating professional competence in command.

chapters.title: Vietnam and 1st Cavalry Division
chapters.summary: Becton commanded a squadron in the 101st Airborne Division in Vietnam (the only Black commander at that level in the division) where Gen. Creighton Abrams, the MACV commander, made repeat visits to his armored unit. After serving as Deputy Brigade Commander, branch chief for all armor officer assignments, and Deputy Commander at Fort Dix where he lobbied with Mayor Frank L. Rizzo of Philadelphia against the post's closure, Becton was assigned to command the 1st Cavalry Division at Fort Hood in 1976 on Abrams's written instruction. He commanded the division during its conversion to a pure armored configuration.

chapters.title: VII Corps Germany
chapters.summary: Becton commanded VII Corps in Germany, the largest combat corps in the Army, with Gen. George S. Patton IV as his deputy. The Army held his promotion announcement for several months to test how the German government and senior officers would receive a Black corps commander before going public. He delivered his change-of-command speech alternating English and German, having drilled the bilingual translation during German language school in lieu of conversational German. He terminated the 1980 Reforger exercise mid-deployment after a sudden thaw threatened German civilian property, with Supreme Allied Commander Alexander Haig (a Lower Merion classmate) flying in to deliver the cancellation order.

chapters.title: USAID, FEMA, and DCPS
chapters.summary: After retiring as a Lieutenant General in August 1983, Becton directed disaster assistance at USAID where he coordinated the U.S. response to the Ethiopian famine, then served as Director of FEMA under Reagan and Bush. His refusal to approve New Hampshire Governor John H. Sununu's Seabrook nuclear plant evacuation plan led Sununu (now Bush's chief of staff) to force him out in 1989. Bush offered an ambassadorship to a Caribbean nation which Louise refused. Becton later served as Superintendent of DC Public Schools during the financial control board era, departing after 18 months at his family's insistence, and chaired the Military Leadership Diversity Commission that opened combat positions and Ranger School to women.

loc_video.caption: Julius W. Becton oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Ft. Belvoir, Virginia, 2015 December 15
